label: Apple Music Catalog by ID
summary: Apple's official catalog song endpoints expose durationInMillis directly, which makes this a high-trust SongMetadata duration enricher when the accepted match already preserves Apple song identity.
Unlike public iTunes lookup, this path avoids broad search ambiguity and should become the stronger Apple-backed lane once Axolync can own Apple credentials honestly.
The real cost is operational and architectural rather than conceptual: developer-token handling and runtime placement dominate the implementation decision.

signals: Apple's official catalog song APIs expose durationInMillis directly.
Real GitHub integrations exist around MusicKit / Apple Music API flows, proving this is an honest implementation family rather than a hypothetical path.
This lane is quality-first but auth-heavy, so its real fit depends on whether Axolync wants host-brokered tokens or a native Apple bridge.

notes: The decisive dependency is Apple's hosted catalog API and developer-program terms, even when community wrappers are used.

entries.subject: Apple Music API Songs
notes: The adapter depends on Apple Music API access and the associated Apple developer and MusicKit requirements.

entries.subject: rryam/MusadoraKit
notes: Community SDK evidence shows this catalog path is implementable through open-source tooling even though the real authority stays with Apple's service.
The API semantics are strong and stable compared with scrape-style alternatives.
Credential handling, token refresh, and runtime placement are the real long-term maintenance cost.

methods.notes: Feasible with browser-capable auth flows, but token handling and Apple configuration make this less clean than public lookup or a host-owned token lane.
There is no engine to port; the problem is auth and HTTP orchestration.
A native wrapper can own Apple platform credentials while exposing only the normalized SongMetadata result upward.
Possible but awkward, and still leaves Apple token handling as the real burden.
Too brittle for an authenticated Apple metadata lane.
A companion could own credentials, but it is still heavier than a shared host or native bridge.
Python can broker the HTTP calls, but there is no particular advantage over Node here.
Clean server-side token ownership and straightforward HTTP make this one of the strongest implementation shapes.
A CLI layer is possible, but weaker than a long-lived host that can cache and reuse tokens.
Possible, but usually less direct than using a host runtime or real platform bridge.
There is nothing to convert; the hard part is the auth story.
A shared service is an honest way to centralize Apple credentials and cache catalog responses.
Feasible, but more operationally expensive than a local shared host for current Axolync scope.
Apple-native SDK paths are real here, especially if Axolync eventually leans into native wrapper lanes.

notes: This is a very strong Apple-backed duration enricher once the platform is ready to own Apple credentials honestly.
It should outrank public iTunes search/lookup when the accepted match already preserves Apple song identity.

openQuestions: Will accepted SongSense matches preserve Apple song ids directly enough to justify this as a first implemented Apple adapter rather than a later hardening lane?
Should Axolync prefer a server/host token broker or a wrapper-native Apple Music lane first?
